sentences.text: OK, I'm coming from a very, very traditional Jewish home with a beautiful family.
We were four people.
We were four children, my father and mother.
We had strong Jewish values.
I was-- I went to a Polish-- to a school, to a public school.
And every day after school, I went to a Hebrew school.
And life was beautiful.
We had a big family, lots of cousins, and we used to visit our grandma ever Saturday.
We had beautiful, traditional dinners, Friday night with families, holidays.
It was just beautiful.
I have one living brother, and I have one living sister who lives in Israel.
I had a little sister, she was 11 years old, and she was too young to live.
She was taken away in 1942 from the Lodz ghetto.
The family was just beautiful.
I had lots of cousin.
It was a long-rooted family in Poland, maybe 200 years.
My name, before the war, was Cichanowski.
And this is coming back from very, very known people.
And what can I say?
I was young.
We were happy.
We were together.
We had a family visiting.
We lived in a suburb of Lodz.
We had visiting family from Lodz, from the big city, for the summer, for the holidays.
We played outside.
We were children.
I was all about 15 when that war started.
What did your father do?
My father was a very successful businessman.
He was very active in the Jewish community.
He was very involved in Hebrew school.
He was very involved in temple, and he was big Zionist.
And my mother was a beautiful lady.
She was a housewife and kept a beautiful home.
We were manufacturing English yacht goods.
This was a small city, the name was Konstantynow, near Lodz.
It was about seven miles from Lodz.
I knew two of my grandmothers.
They were beautiful.
In my eyes, they looked old, but they probably were very young, because I'm going back now 50 years, and I was 15.
So maybe my grandma was maybe 50.
But they were beautiful ladies.
I remember I used to go to Shabbat, and she always set for us food and cookies and fruit.
It was just a loving family.
I never liked my school.
I always felt that I was fairly discriminated being Jewish, because my math teacher always used to call out my name, Masza.
And Masza was a really a Jewish name, not a Polish name.
And I always hated this.
In my mind, I always felt when I was very young, if I ever, ever grow up, I'm going to leave this country.
And I'm going to go far away where I can do whatever I want.
We were always afraid to go out to play because they were always throwing stones at us.
I was always going with my older brother Leon.
He was my protector.
We had to walk to school.
School was maybe a mile from home, and I was a pretty good student.
I finished-- I was, my education was a eight grade, and [SIGHS] I really don't remember much more.
When we were-- I was young, I was going swimming with my brother and with my friends, and the Polish boys, they hated us.
They always hated the Jews.
We were always felt to be discriminated, so they would-- I would never go alone without my brother.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
I was always afraid.
After-- after--
[SIGHS] Before the war.
Well, [SIGHS] I loved to go to my Hebrew school.
I was very, very much involved in Hebrew school.
I spent mostly Saturdays and Sundays being in Hebrew school and studying Hebrew and studying the Torah.
I was very, very happy to be there.
I felt very, very relieved and safe.
And I made-- I had beautiful, beautiful friends.
The girls were just beautiful.
And it was a beautiful life.
We didn't know any different.
After 1939 the war broke out, and [SIGHS] all of the sudden-- it was about September-- we heard Germans came in to our city.
We got very confused, everybody was running.
My father gathered the children, and we ran.
We left from our city.
We went to Lodz.
And [SIGHS] we left just the way we were dressed, with nothing.
And after a while my father said, where are we running?
Let's go back.
So we went back home.
I remember when I was [SIGHS] maybe at that time 14, 15, I was curious, and it was when then I walked out in the street and was wearing a coat, like a fur lining inside.
And all of a sudden, a young German came, and he said, give me your coat.
He took off the coat, the middle of the winter.
I came home crying, and then it just-- that was it.
So after the Germans came in, we stayed.
It was-- life was very uncertain.
We were scared.
We were caught to work.
I remember I was caught to work.
I was very young, 15.
They took us to do the hardest work, like cleaning post offices, cleaning schools, cleaning street.
And all of a sudden, like the end of November, they round up all the Jews to a marketplace to deport them.
And I wasn't home, and I thought, I would never, never see my parents again.
But, at the last minute, they released me from work, and I met my parents at the place where we were gathered.
And just the way I was dressed with nothing, they told us [SOBBING] to give all the money, all the jewelry, all our possessions.
Poland was divided to a reich and protectorate.
They sent us to the protectorate, which was about maybe 100 miles from the city I was born.
It was very cold.
Old people who were dying.
Young children were freezing.
We were marching for days and days until we got to the protectorate.
We came, it was a city.
I don't know how you say it in Polish, it was Lowicz.
A lot of people were gathered in the city.
It was very cold with no food, epidemic, dirt, no facilities.
People were dying of typhus.
After a while, I was young, and I saw that's not the life for me, so I remembered I have relatives in Lodz, so I went to the train.
And I jumped a train and went back to a Lodz.
All of a sudden, on the train-- it was my sister, my youngest sister and I.
All of a sudden I see on the train, it was a lot of a panic going on.
So I realized that the Germans came and they wanted to get all the Jews.
So I jumped a train.
It was maybe 10 miles before Lodz, and I walked into Lodz-- With your-- with your sister, or by yourself?
With my sister.
We jumped a train, I don't know how I did it.
I guess when you're young, you don't know what you're doing.
You just do it.
In panic, you do it.
I saw people are jumping, so I jumped, too.
And we walked into Lodz.
We had a family in Lodz, my cousin Masza and her family.
Which, she had five siblings, and she is the youngest.
She survived.
We were hiding.
And we stayed in Lodz.
After a while, I don't know how, my mother and father came to Lodz.
When my mother came, she could not jump the train, so the Germans rounded her up and guarded her with German Shepherds.
She stood all night in 10 degrees below zero.
She came to Lodz [SOBBING] frozen-- frozen hands, frozen legs.
[SOBBING] I could not recognize the way she looked.
After a while, we reunited.
And [SIGHS] they gave us a little apartment in the ghetto.
[BACKROUND NOISE] It had no floors, had no windows, had no facilities.
Nobody had a bathroom, nobody had running water.
But at least we all were together.
Going back-- I have to go back. 1939, a friend of my father's came to my father and said, I went to a city meeting, and they saying that they going to deport all the young people.
So my father took my brother which was 18 years old, and the German friend got him down the stairs.
We lived on the first floor.
Got him out from the city, and he was heading to the Russian border.
This was still before, this was in 1939.
He got him out, and I remembered that my father and mother stood on the top of the stairs and they-- they said, just play cool.
Don't say goodbye, because it will be too obvious.
Well, he left to Russia, and he was there for six years.
We didn't know where he was alive or he was dead.
We couldn't hear because the war started.
The Germans occupied the first cities in Russia and thanks God he survived, but we did not hear from him for about six years. 1941, my mother and father, it was no food in ghetto.
Absolutely nothing.
Even the Jewish people managed to get a very, very poorly.
Before the food got to the people, everything was rotten.
So they died very young of starvation.
I was still there with my youngest sister, who was 10 years old at the time, and my youngest sister, who was two years younger than I am, Guta.
And my mother died before, and [SIGHS] it was cold.
It was in the winter.
It was probably December.
They-- people were dying of typhus.
There were so many deaths, they couldn't bury them fast enough.
So I remember I slept with my mother's corpus in the same house for days till they removed her.
My cousin, who lived in the same building, went over and said to my little sister, who was 10, aren't you afraid to stay with the corpus in the house?
She said no, why should I be afraid?
This is my mother.
Well, I don't remember how many days it took, but they took her away.
Then, about a few days later, my father died of starvation.
There was no food whatsoever.
The conditions were terrible.
We had no food, no water.
It was very cold.
I remember when I went-- when I had to wash my hair, I had long, beautiful, black hair.
We bought a little water.
One in the building, maybe 50 tenants, had the little gas machine.
And I bought like a glass of water and washed my hair.
By the time I washed my hair, and I combed down snow.
It was freezing.
Times [SIGHS] were just terrible.
I just don't know.
They took away everything from us.
They took our identity.
They took away our pride.
They thought that we are animals.
We had no facilities when we had to go to the bathroom.
We had to go wherever we went.
They make pictures that we are animals.
We don't know how to behave, that we don't know what it means, bathroom.
[SIGHS] At this time, I was then left with my youngest sister, who was two years younger than I am.
She was at that time 14.
And we stayed in ghetto.
Then they start to establish working places.
And I went to work in a factory.
They manufactured German uniforms.
I was very, very talented, so was my sister, so they gave us a job to work on the German-- how you call, we made-- we made the hand stitches on the-- I don't know how you call it.
It was a very, very delicate work.
And we worked in the factory, and we got just a little bit of soup and a little piece of bread.
And the-- the ghetto was divided.
We could not cross the street, so they made bridges from one street [BACKROUND NOISE] to the other, so we had to walk the bridge in the winter.
We had no shoes.
This was a few years later.
So we were wearing wooden shoes, and I remember the snow got stuck to the shoes.
So one she was high and one shoe was short.
It was so hard to walk.
But this I'm going back too late, I have to go back.
They closed the ghetto in 1940.
And we could never, never get out anymore from the ghetto.
[SIGHS] Let me think.
But I was still, in 1940, with my parents till 1941, when they passed away.
But then I got-- when I was with my parents, I was working.
And the one who worked got a little ration, like one soup a day.
But I remember that my parents, they were too sick to work, so I ate like every third day and brought my soup home for my father and mother to survive.
Remember when my father died, his last words were, [SOBBING] will I ever, ever see my son?
He couldn't even say goodbye to his son when he left home.
And then he said to us children, if you ever, ever survive, don't stay in Poland.
Leave.
There is a better world outside.
And I remember when he died.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
And then I was left only with my younger sister. 1942, they rounded up all the Jews in the ghetto, and they had a great selection.
And they took out most of the people.
At that time, they took out my younger sister, who was 12 years old.
She was too young to live.
So they took her, and they killed her.
It was terrible.
It was so confusing.
We didn't know what to do.
Lucky that I was still looking pretty good.
So they could use with me for work.
So we stayed.
Well, afterwards, I went to work, and I was working in a factory.
Got one soup a day, and I worked in that factory till 1944. 1944, the Germans came, and they round up the ghetto and send everybody out.
We didn't know, it was a terrible confusion.
We didn't know where we were going.
But they sent-- they round them up and they sent them to Auschwitz.
They closed the Lodz ghetto.
I was hiding with my sister in a basement, and [SIGHS] all day, we were hiding.
In the daytime, we wake up-- at night, excuse me, we were coming out for fresh air.
We were so weak, no food.
My sister used to say to me, leave me.
I can't do it anymore.
I said, no, you going where I'm going.
As long as I will survive, you going to survive.
She said, no, leave me alone.
Anyway, it was 1944, they liquidated the Lodz ghetto, they took us all to-- on a train to Auschwitz.
[SIGHS] I guess, we were very young and we survived somehow.
When you're young, we survive.
Everybody was taken away in ghetto.
After my younger sister was taken away in ghetto-- this was 1944-- I was sitting at the windows and I turned gray.
I was staring to the window, and [SIGHS] I just didn't know what was happening to me.
I said, now it's no use.
I don't want to live anymore, no use.
But somehow we stayed another year till everybody was deported from Lodz ghetto to Auschwitz.
My sister and and--
I still stayed with my sister.
I was with her all the years till we got in 1944, till we got into Auschwitz.
Everybody was killed right in the beginning, everybody.
There were round-ups.
We never knew what's going to be next day.
We never-- we lived from day to day, we never knew what's going to be next minute.
Who's going to be caught and who's going to be killed.
Nothing, nothing.
We were not allowed to have any telephones.
We didn't have telephones in Poland at that time.
But there was no news.
We could not get any letters.
We were cut off from the whole world.
Was absolutely nothing, no news.
We just lived from day to day, and we were hoping that we will survive.
There was not a thing we could do to survive.
Whenever they want, they caught us and sent us to extermination camps.
Life in the ghetto was terrible.
We had no facilities.
We had no food.
We had no fuel.
Poland was very, very cold.
It was very, very cold.
We had no communication with each other.
We were in a ghetto with barbed wires.
The barbed wires, we had SS every few miles.
They were patrolling like we could run.
Where could we run?
We were so weak and so weak there was no place to run.
And we were treated just [SIGHS] like we were animals-- even worse than animals.
Was no food, we didn't know anything what's happening in the world.
I had a cousin living in the same court with her whole family.
They were a family of five children, but [SIGHS] they were taking-- we were-- had every few months round-ups in ghetto.
And they took people out till it got thinner, and thinner, and thinner.
My cousin who survived, now she lives in Chicago.
She's my age so she was hiding all by herself in my-- in my place, and [BACKROUND NOISE] somehow we survive.
And her whole family [BACKROUND NOISE] was taken away at round-- round-up in 1942.
My cousin survived.
Now she lives in Chicago.
And we had no food.
The little food I had, I shared it with her and we survived.
Every single day I went to the factory--
--and I worked.
And we had-- whoever worked got a ration of a little bit of soup.
And who didn't work didn't get any food.
Just in the factory with the Jewish people.
We were guarded all the time.
We could not speak.
We could not see anybody.
We had no schools.
We just went to work, and we came home.
We went to work and came home.
Every day for four years.
No.
Never, never.
We just were like animals.
Went to work, got our soup, went home, went to sleep.
Got up in the morning.
Went to work.
I was very young and very talented, so they used me to make the Germans-- how you call what they wear on the shoulders?
The epaulets.
I was in like in a crib with all the gold threads, and I made the epaulets.
They used me because I'm very good with my hands.
And so was my sister.
We were [SIGHS] in the Lodz ghetto-- they close the ghetto, 1940 till 1944, around September.
And every day was going to work, coming home, going to work, for 12, 14 hours a day with one little bit of soup.
No schools, nothing.
No.
No way, no way.
